title: Sleeping Gods - Traversing a Sea of Wonder
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=sZhgf8PQ5gc

Daniel (@dungeondive) — pipe in hand, whiskey poured — delivers his preliminary thoughts on Red Raven's Sleeping Gods, and he is unguardedly smitten. Framed by a stressful real-life sailing trip across the Puget Sound, he explains the longing this game finally satisfies: the illusion of true exploration, of charting a course into unknown waters and not knowing what you'll find. He calls it, without hesitation, a masterpiece and a top-ten game of all time.

He praises the gold-standard out-of-box experience — quick-start guide, a beautifully written rulebook, and a Universal Head rules summary that instantly earns his trust (he flags one repeated erratum: the four starting cards live in the market deck, not the adventure deck). You captain the nine-strong crew of the Manticore, teleported to a strange open sea, questing across an atlas to gather totems and wake the sleeping gods. A Book of Tales delivers short, sharp, agonising choices, with keywords unlocking divergent branches. Skill tests use a clever fate/ability deck rather than dice; command tokens, fatigue, wounds and cooked recipes are the resources.

Crucially, there's no grinding survival upkeep — the ship has fuel for the whole campaign — so every turn pushes the fun forward. Combat is a brutal spatial puzzle of covering damage and splashing it onto neighbours.

----

Have you found a game that genuinely captures the romance of open-sea exploration — or is that feeling, as Daniel suggests, almost impossible to deliver?
